question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: isHealthy / take] Can an unprivileged seller exploit the fact that isHealthy is called at the end of take (after the seller callback) and the oracle is called inside isHealthy for each activated collateral, allowing a reentrant oracle to call take again (for the same seller) during the health check, increasing the seller's debt in the reentrant call before the outer health check completes, so the outer health check sees the pre-reentrant debt but the final state has higher debt? Preconditions: seller's activated collateral oracle is a reentrant contract; seller callback triggers oracle reentry into take. Call sequence: take(seller=A, sellerCallback=X) -> isHealthy(A) -> oracle.price() reenters take(seller=A) -> seller.debt increases -> outer isHealthy uses stale debt. Invariant: every borrow/take must create correct debt and collateral obligation. Scoped impact: bad debt — seller accumulates more debt than health check validated. Proof idea: deploy ReentrantOracle that calls take in price(); assert seller.debt matches health check at end of outer take.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the RCF dust check uses collateral[collateralIndex].mulDivDown(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(WAD, lif).zeroFloorSub(maxRepaid) < rcfThreshold, and when liquidatedCollatPrice is very small, this expression always evaluates to 0 (due to mulDivDown rounding to 0), making the dust condition always true and deactivating the RCF for any repaidUnits, allowing full liquidation of a position that should only be partially liquidated? Preconditions: oracle returns price = 1 (minimum); rcfThreshold > 0; position is unhealthy but not fully undercollateralized. Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> take -> oracle.price() = 1 -> liquidate(repaidUnits=position.debt). Invariant: repay amount capped by recovery close factor. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure — full liquidation when only partial should be allowed. Proof idea: fuzz (price, collateral, rcfThreshold); assert RCF is not bypassed when collateral value > rcfThreshold.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that badDebt uses zeroFloorSub across all collaterals (badDebt = originalDebt - sum(collateral[i].mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, maxLif))), and in a multi-collateral market, one collateral with a very high oracle price can make badDebt = 0 even when the position is deeply undercollateralized on other collaterals, preventing bad debt socialization and leaving lenders with unrecoverable credit? Preconditions: multi-collateral market; one collateral has inflated oracle price; other collaterals are worthless; position is unhealthy. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(index=0, oracle=inflated) -> supplyCollateral(index=1, oracle=zero) -> take -> liquidate(collateralIndex=1). Invariant: bad debt must reduce lender credit exactly once and proportionally. Scoped impact: insolvency — bad debt not socialized, lender credit unrecoverable. Proof idea: construct scenario where sum(mulDivUp) >= debt but actual recoverable value < debt; assert badDebt > 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the ordering of operations in liquidate — bad debt realized first (reducing _position.debt), then seize/repay computed — to call liquidate with repaidUnits = 0 and seizedAssets = 0 to realize bad debt, then immediately call liquidate again with repaidUnits > 0 on the same position, where the second call's maxDebt computation uses the same oracle prices but the debt is now lower (post-badDebt), potentially making the position appear healthy and reverting with NotLiquidatable, blocking further liquidation? Preconditions: position has badDebt > 0; liquidator calls liquidate twice. Call sequence: liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) -> debt reduced by badDebt -> liquidate(repaidUnits > 0) -> NotLiquidatable. Invariant: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable after bad debt realization. Scoped impact: fund freeze — remaining debt cannot be liquidated after bad debt step. Proof idea: assert second liquidate succeeds when remaining debt > maxDebt after badDebt reduction.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that in post-maturity mode the RCF is deactivated (no maxRepaid check), and the seizedAssets computation seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice) with lif = WAD (at maturity+1) and a very low oracle price, cause seizedAssets to exceed the borrower's collateral[collateralIndex], triggering an underflow in newCollateral = _position.collateral[collateralIndex] - seizedAssets? Preconditions: post-maturity mode; oracle returns low price; repaidUnits chosen such that seizedAssets > collateral[collateralIndex]. Call sequence: warp to maturity+1 -> liquidate(postMaturityMode=true, repaidUnits=large). Invariant: seized assets must not exceed available collateral. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — collateral underflows. Proof idea: fuzz (repaidUnits, price) in post-maturity; assert seizedAssets <= collateral[collateralIndex].

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: isHealthy / withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower exploit the fact that withdrawCollateral reduces collateral BEFORE calling isHealthy (line 561-568), and isHealthy calls oracle.price() for all activated collaterals, so if the oracle for a non-withdrawn collateral returns a temporarily inflated price during the health check, the borrower can withdraw collateral that would leave the position unhealthy at the true oracle price? Preconditions: oracle for non-withdrawn collateral is manipulable (e.g., AMM-based); borrower has debt; borrower withdraws collateral when oracle is inflated. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(index=0, oracle=AMM) -> supplyCollateral(index=1) -> take -> manipulate AMM -> withdrawCollateral(index=1). Invariant: collateral cannot be withdrawn if it would make debt unhealthy. Scoped impact: bad debt — borrower withdraws collateral leaving position undercollateralized at true price. Proof idea: deploy AMM oracle; manipulate price in same tx as withdrawCollateral; assert health at true price.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function does NOT check that collateralIndex < market.collateralParams.length, and passing an out-of-bounds collateralIndex causes market.collateralParams[collateralIndex] to access uninitialized memory (returning zero-value CollateralParams with oracle=address(0)), causing IOracle(address(0)).price() to revert or return unexpected data, corrupting the liquidation? Preconditions: market has N collaterals; liquidator passes collateralIndex >= N. Call sequence: createMarket(2 collaterals) -> supplyCollateral(index=0) -> take -> liquidate(collateralIndex=5). Invariant: liquidation must use valid collateral parameters. Scoped impact: fund freeze or accounting corruption — liquidation reverts or uses wrong oracle. Proof idea: assert liquidate reverts with a meaningful error when collateralIndex >= market.collateralParams.length.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the seize path uses mulDivUp for repaidUnits (repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif)) while the repay path uses mulDivDown for seizedAssets (seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice)), creating an asymmetry where seize-then-compute-repay gives a different repaidUnits than repay-then-compute-seize for the same economic position, allowing the liquidator to choose the path that extracts more value? Preconditions: position is unhealthy; oracle price at rounding boundary. Call sequence: liquidate(seizedAssets=X) vs liquidate(repaidUnits=Y) where X and Y are economically equivalent. Invariant: seize and repay amounts must be mutually consistent. Scoped impact: direct loss — liquidator extracts excess value by choosing favorable rounding path. Proof idea: for each (price, lif, amount), assert seize-path and repay-path give equivalent economic outcomes within 1 unit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the bitmap loop in liquidate processes collaterals from highest index to lowest (msb first), and if the oracle for the highest-index collateral returns a price that makes badDebt = 0 via zeroFloorSub, but the oracle for the lowest-index collateral (the one being liquidated) returns a price that makes the seize computation unfavorable, the liquidator can choose to liquidate the highest-index collateral instead to get a better seize ratio while badDebt is still 0? Preconditions: multi-collateral market; different oracle prices for different collaterals; position is unhealthy. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(multiple) -> take -> liquidate(collateralIndex=highest_value_index). Invariant: liquidation incentive must be consistent across collateral choices. Scoped impact: direct loss — liquidator extracts maximum value by cherry-picking collateral. Proof idea: assert that liquidating any collateral leaves the position in a consistent state.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the post-maturity LIF ramp uses integer division (block.timestamp - market.maturity) / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF without rounding up, so at block.timestamp = market.maturity + TIME_TO_MAX_LIF - 1, lif = WAD + (maxLif - WAD) * (TIME_TO_MAX_LIF - 1) / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF which is slightly less than maxLif, and combined with oracle price rounding, cause the seizedAssets to be 1 wei less than expected, leaving dust collateral that prevents the position from being fully liquidated? Preconditions: post-maturity mode; block.timestamp near maturity + TIME_TO_MAX_LIF; oracle price at rounding boundary. Call sequence: warp to maturity + TIME_TO_MAX_LIF - 1 -> liquidate(postMaturityMode=true, repaidUnits=position.debt). Invariant: full liquidation must be achievable when residual collateral is below dust. Scoped impact: fund freeze — dust collateral prevents full liquidation. Proof idea: fuzz elapsed time in [0, TIME_TO_MAX_LIF]; assert full liquidation possible when collateral value < rcfThreshold.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function emits the Liquidate event BEFORE the callback and token transfers, and the event includes badDebt and lossFactor values, allowing an oracle that monitors events to react to the emitted lossFactor and return a different price in the subsequent liquidateCallback, corrupting the accounting of a second liquidation triggered within the callback? Preconditions: oracle monitors Midnight events; liquidateCallback triggers a second liquidation; oracle adjusts price based on emitted lossFactor. Call sequence: liquidate(callback=malicious) -> Liquidate event emitted -> onLiquidate callback -> oracle.price() returns new value -> second liquidate. Invariant: callbacks must not corrupt partial state through reentrancy. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — lossFactor or withdrawable desync. Proof idea: deploy event-monitoring oracle; assert marketState invariants after nested liquidate in callback.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: isHealthy / take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the seller's health check in take calls isHealthy which calls oracle.price() for all activated collaterals, and if the oracle for one collateral is a contract that calls back into take (for a different offer/market), the reentrant take modifies the seller's position (e.g., reduces debt via buying units), causing the outer health check to see a different debt value than what was computed at the start of the outer take? Preconditions: seller has activated collateral with reentrant oracle; reentrant take reduces seller's debt. Call sequence: take(seller=A) -> isHealthy(A) -> oracle.price() reenters take(buyer=A, reducing debt) -> outer isHealthy sees reduced debt -> passes. Invariant: health check must reflect the state at the end of the outer take. Scoped impact: bad debt — seller's debt reduction in reentrant call not reflected in outer health check. Proof idea: deploy ReentrantOracle that calls take in price(); assert seller.debt and health are consistent after outer take.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the maxRepaid formula uses _position.debt (post-badDebt) and maxDebt (computed in the same loop), and when LLTV = 1e18 (WAD), maxRepaid = type(uint256).max (RCF deactivated), allowing the liquidator to pass repaidUnits = type(uint256).max, causing _position.debt -= type(uint256).max to underflow and wrap around to a large value, corrupting the borrower's debt accounting? Preconditions: market with LLTV = 1e18; position is unhealthy; liquidator calls liquidate(repaidUnits=type(uint256).max). Call sequence: createMarket(lltv=WAD) -> supplyCollateral -> take -> liquidate(repaidUnits=type(uint256).max). Invariant: debt accounting must remain consistent. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — borrower.debt wraps to large value. Proof idea: assert _position.debt - repaidUnits does not underflow when repaidUnits > _position.debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the collateralBitmap is cleared (clearBit) when newCollateral == 0 after seizing, but the oracle is still called for that index in the SAME bitmap loop iteration (price already fetched before clearBit), and if the oracle for that index is a contract that checks whether the bit is set in the bitmap to determine its price, it returns a different price than expected, corrupting maxDebt and badDebt computations? Preconditions: oracle checks collateralBitmap state; liquidator seizes all collateral at one index. Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> take -> liquidate(seizedAssets=collateral[i]) -> oracle checks bitmap mid-loop. Invariant: oracle price must be consistent within a single liquidation call. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — maxDebt or badDebt computed with wrong price. Proof idea: deploy BitmapCheckingOracle; assert maxDebt and badDebt are consistent regardless of bitmap state during loop.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function does not validate that the oracle address in CollateralParams is non-zero, and a market created with oracle=address(0) for a collateral will cause IOracle(address(0)).price() to return 0 (EXTCALL to zero address returns empty data, which ABI-decodes to 0), making maxDebt += 0 for that collateral, allowing the liquidator to liquidate a position that is only healthy because of that collateral's value? Preconditions: market created with oracle=address(0) for one collateral; borrower uses that collateral; position appears unhealthy due to oracle returning 0. Call sequence: createMarket(oracle=address(0)) -> supplyCollateral -> take -> liquidate. Invariant: oracle must return correct price for health and liquidation computations. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure — healthy borrower liquidated due to zero oracle. Proof idea: assert that call to address(0) returns 0 in Solidity 0.8.34; assert liquidation of position backed by address(0) oracle is blocked.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the continuousFeeCredit slashing in liquidate uses the NEW lossFactor (_marketState.lossFactor after update) in the formula continuousFeeCredit.mulDivDown(type(uint128).max - newLossFactor, type(uint128).max - oldLossFactor), and if badDebt is very small relative to totalUnits, the new lossFactor barely changes, causing continuousFeeCredit to be slashed by a negligible amount, allowing the fee claimer to later claim more continuousFeeCredit than the remaining lender credit supports? Preconditions: large totalUnits; small badDebt; continuousFeeCredit > 0; liquidator calls liquidate. Call sequence: take (large credit) -> take (small borrow) -> oracle.price() = 0 -> liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) -> claimContinuousFee. Invariant: continuousFeeCredit must not exceed proportional share of remaining lender credit. Scoped impact: insolvency — fee claimer withdraws more than remaining lender credit supports. Proof idea: assert continuousFeeCredit / totalUnits ratio is preserved after bad debt realization.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: isHealthy / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that isHealthy and liquidate both compute maxDebt independently using the same oracle, but if the oracle is a contract with block-level state (e.g., returns different values in different calls within the same block), the maxDebt in isHealthy (called at end of take) and the maxDebt in liquidate (called in the next transaction) can differ, allowing a position that passed the health check in take to be immediately liquidatable in the next transaction without any price change? Preconditions: oracle returns different values per call within same block; position passes health check in take; liquidator calls liquidate in same block. Call sequence: take (seller borrows) -> isHealthy passes with price P1 -> liquidate in same block with price P2 < P1. Invariant: position health must be consistent between take and liquidate. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure — borrower liquidated immediately after borrowing. Proof idea: deploy CallCountOracle returning decreasing prices; assert position cannot be liquidated in same block as take.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function uses market.collateralParams[collateralIndex].maxLif for the lif computation in post-maturity mode, but the bitmap loop uses market.collateralParams[i].maxLif for badDebt computation, and if the market was created with different maxLif values for different collaterals, the liquidator can choose a collateralIndex with a high maxLif (low LIF incentive) to minimize repaidUnits while the badDebt computation uses a low maxLif (high LIF) for other collaterals, extracting more collateral per unit of debt repaid? Preconditions: multi-collateral market with different maxLif values; position is unhealthy; liquidator chooses collateralIndex strategically. Call sequence: createMarket(collateral[0].maxLif=HIGH, collateral[1].maxLif=LOW) -> supplyCollateral(both) -> take -> liquidate(collateralIndex=0). Invariant: liquidation incentive must be consistent with market parameters. Scoped impact: direct loss — liquidator extracts excess collateral relative to debt repaid. Proof idea: assert seizedAssets / repaidUnits ratio matches lif for chosen collateralIndex.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function's bitmap loop calls IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() as an external call with no gas limit, allowing a malicious oracle to consume all remaining gas and cause the liquidation to revert with out-of-gas, permanently blocking liquidation of an unhealthy position? Preconditions: oracle is a gas-consuming contract (e.g., infinite loop with gas check); position is unhealthy; liquidator calls liquidate with limited gas. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(oracle=GasConsumer) -> take -> liquidate (out of gas). Invariant: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable. Scoped impact: fund freeze — liquidation permanently blocked by gas griefing. Proof idea: deploy GasConsumingOracle; assert liquidate can complete within block gas limit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the withdrawable accounting in liquidate (_marketState.withdrawable += repaidUnits) is updated BEFORE the callback and token transfer, and the liquidateCallback can call withdraw to drain the newly added withdrawable before the repaidUnits token transfer from the liquidator completes, causing the contract to send loan tokens to a lender without receiving the repaidUnits from the liquidator? Preconditions: liquidator uses a callback; callback calls withdraw for a lender; token transfer from liquidator happens after callback. Call sequence: liquidate(callback=malicious) -> withdrawable += repaidUnits -> onLiquidate callback -> withdraw(repaidUnits) -> safeTransfer to lender -> safeTransferFrom liquidator. Invariant: contract token balances must cover withdrawable assets. Scoped impact: insolvency — loan tokens sent to lender before liquidator pays. Proof idea: assert contract.balanceOf(loanToken) >= 0 after liquidate with draining callback.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the badDebt computation uses zeroFloorSub(originalDebt, sum(mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, maxLif))), and with LLTV=1e18 (WAD), maxLif = WAD (since maxLif(WAD, cursor) = WAD*WAD/(WAD - cursor*(WAD-WAD)/WAD) = WAD), so badDebt = originalDebt - sum(collateral[i].mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, WAD)) = originalDebt - sum(collateral[i].mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE)), meaning any collateral with price > 0 reduces badDebt, potentially making badDebt = 0 even when the position is deeply unhealthy? Preconditions: market with LLTV=1e18; position is unhealthy (debt > maxDebt); oracle returns non-zero price. Call sequence: createMarket(lltv=WAD) -> supplyCollateral -> take -> price drops -> liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0). Invariant: bad debt must be realized for genuinely undercollateralized positions. Scoped impact: insolvency — bad debt not socialized for LLTV=1 markets. Proof idea: assert badDebt > 0 when debt > sum(collateral[i] * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) for LLTV=1.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function does not check that repaidUnits <= _position.debt (after badDebt subtraction), and passing repaidUnits > remaining debt causes _position.debt -= repaidUnits to underflow (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic would revert, but toUint128 is used), triggering a CastOverflow revert that permanently blocks liquidation of the position? Preconditions: position has small remaining debt after badDebt; liquidator passes repaidUnits > remaining debt. Call sequence: liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) -> debt reduced by badDebt -> liquidate(repaidUnits=originalDebt). Invariant: debt accounting must remain consistent. Scoped impact: fund freeze — liquidation reverts, position stuck. Proof idea: assert liquidate reverts with meaningful error (not CastOverflow) when repaidUnits > remaining debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: Critical Direct loss of user funds through incorrect credit, debt, collateral, withdrawable, or fee accounting'] [Function: isHealthy / liquidate] Can an unprivileged market creator deploy a market where two different collateral indices share the same oracle address, and the oracle is a stateful contract that returns different prices on successive calls within the same transaction, causing isHealthy and liquidate to compute different maxDebt values for the same position in the same block, creating an exploitable window where the position is healthy in isHealthy but unhealthy in liquidate? Preconditions: two collaterals share same oracle; oracle returns P1 on first call and P2 < P1 on second call; position is healthy at P1 but unhealthy at P2. Call sequence: createMarket(collateral[0].oracle == collateral[1].oracle) -> supplyCollateral(both) -> take -> liquidate in same block. Invariant: health check must be consistent between isHealthy and liquidate. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure — healthy borrower liquidated due to oracle state change. Proof idea: deploy DecreasingOracle; assert liquidate(postMaturityMode=false) reverts with NotLiquidatable when isHealthy returns true.
